# Robert Voss (US History)

<!-- vimeo: 1111476058 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

<!-- vid-ref:1111476058 -->
[▶ Watch on Vimeo](https://vimeo.com/1111476058)


I am Dr. Robert Voss, an associate professor of history and the social science education coordinator at Northwest Missouri State University. Before I moved into higher education, I was a high school social studies teacher, so my teaching roots are very much in the classroom. I've been technology first, my entire teaching career. I still remember the sound of a 2000 BOD modem connecting. It's not a pleasant sound, but for me it was a sound of possibility. In the late nineties when I was earning my teaching credential, I was the one with a Sony digital camera that saved pictures on a 3.5 inch floppy disc. I was building websites with second graders in 1998. I had students using Palm Pilots years before the first iPhone came out. So technology for me was never just a tool, it was a way to expand what was possible in the classroom. And then AI came along and everything shifted. When I first started using chat GBT in November of 2022, I asked it to generate a lesson plan to teach AI to second graders. It got it right on the first prompt, and I was sold. My whole approach moved from, moved from technology first to AI first. 'cause AI isn't just another tool out on the out of the box, it's a superpower. See, I was diagnosed with A DHD as an adult, which means my brain loves chasing ideas and add that to my natural curiosity that can be challenging. My brain doesn't always keep these ideas neatly in one place. With ai, however, my conversations are recorded, I can click back to a previous thread and pick up exactly where I left off. For me and my neurodivergent students. That's not just faster, it's a way to make thinking more organized and more accessible. When I started bringing AI into my classes, I treat treated it like a collaborator. I built custom GPT to act as research assistants that help students navigate primary source analysis, locate relevant archives, or compare historical perspectives. I designed assignments for, uh, for students AI to translate handwritten civil war letters or 19th century Swedish immigration records, and then interpret those sources in their own words. In my history courses, AI has even taken on the persona of historical figures so students can interview them in real time. I built these tools by starting with one question. What can AI do here that frees people to spend more time on higher order thinking? I mean, if AI can summarize a a challenging academic article in minutes, we can spend our class time debating its implications instead of simply processing it. I mean, if AI can create a starting point for historical timeline, then students can focus on verifying the details and interpreting the context. Over time. AI has become part of everything I teach in my digital humanities courses. Students learn not just how to use ai, but how to judge it and to evaluate accuracy, it's gaps, and understand bias. In my history survey, students use AI to create comparative timelines, explore historical scenarios, and generate discussion prompts from complex readings. And in our applied ai, minor AI is the foundation from day one. Now, most people don't connect history with ai. They think of coding and robotics or data science. But my work in digital history has always been about bridging traditional research with technology. AI supercharges that connection. As a historian, we are bringing together sources that compare perspectives, analyzing patterns, and building interpretations. And AI can accelerate every one of those steps, but it still needs human expertise to decide what accurate and meaningful AI is allowing us to do the things we could already do, but just faster. And that means my students aren't just reading about the past. They're running AI assisted searches across digitized archives, translating sources from other languages, generating maps and timelines, and even simulating debates between historical figures. AI doesn't replace the historian. It's just a better set of tools in the historian's hands. And here's the best part. I teach at a medium sized rural public university. No graduate assistants, no lab full of high-end computers, and yet AI lets my undergraduates work, do work that would've required a large research team at a major institution just a few years ago. We can analyze large data sets, run multilingual translations, and create interactive digital exhibits all from a single classroom. The result, students are more engaged because the work feels relevant and real class discussions are deeper because AI levels the information playing field, everyone can come prepared with something meaningful to say, I spend less time building repetitive resources and more time responding to student ideas. It's not just my students that benefit either. I've led AI workshops for uh, faculty across disciplines, helping a biology professor with lab data analysis and art professor explore AI for visual inspiration and a fellow historian design interactive debates. I've worked with K 12 educators to align AI to use with Missouri's new state standards. I've spoken at the Missouri Digital Government Summit and a computer science conference about how even schools like ours can lead in AI adoption. And right now I'm leading a grant funded project on how humanities students can best use ai. I mean, humanities often get left out of the AI conversation, but they're exactly where we need to be. Thinking critically about AI's role in society, it turns out that AI is superpowered by the humanities. Thinking critically is an essential skill with ai. We are asking how can a history major use AI to frame better research questions? How can a ledger student use it to find themes across dozens of novels? How can political science students use it to analyze legislative trends? Looking ahead, my goal is to make AI through chat GBT, that AI literacy as fundamental as anything else we want. There we go. We are asking all these very relevant questions. Now, looking ahead, my goal is to make AI as literacy, as fundamental as internet literacy. I want every graduate and every major to use AI effectively and respond to responsibly. And I want faculty to feel confident integrating it into their teaching and research. For me, AI has made my classes more engaging, my students, more capable, and my own thinking sharper. It's taken me from technology first to AI first, and it's helped my students and me achieve things we never imagined possible. For schools like mine for research limited settings, that's not just an upgrade. That's a game changer. And if it can happen here in a small town in Missouri, then it can happen anywhere.
